title: Preamble & Basic Structure Doctrine
subject: Constitutional Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

The Preamble is the soul of the Constitution. It declares India to be a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic securing Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity.

**Key Amendments:** 42nd Amendment (1976) added 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity'.

**Basic Structure Doctrine:** Laid down in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) by a 7-6 majority. Justice H.R. Khanna's decisive opinion held that Parliament's amending power under Article 368 cannot destroy the basic structure.

**Elements of Basic Structure:** Supremacy of Constitution, Rule of Law, Separation of Powers, Judicial Review, Federalism, Secularism, Democracy, Parliamentary System, Free and Fair Elections, Dignity of Individual.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) AIR 1973 SC 1461
- Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975) - Free and fair elections
- Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) - Judicial review is basic structure
- Waman Rao v. Union of India (1981) - Doctrine applies post-Kesavananda
- I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu (2007) - 9th Schedule laws subject to basic structure review

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** 'The Preamble is the key to unlock the mind of the makers of the Constitution.' Discuss with reference to judicial interpretation.
summary: Preamble declares India as Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic. Basic Structure Doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati 1973) limits Parliament's amending power under Article 368.

title: Fundamental Rights - Articles 14 to 18
subject: Constitutional Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Article 14 - Equality Before Law:** Two components: (1) Equality before law - English concept, negative; (2) Equal protection of laws - American concept, positive.

**Test of Reasonable Classification:** (a) Intelligible differentia, (b) Rational nexus with object. State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar (1952).

**New Doctrine:** Anti-arbitrariness - E.P. Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu (1974), Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978).

**Article 15 - Prohibition of Discrimination:** On grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth. Exceptions: Art 15(3) women & children, Art 15(4) & 15(5) socially and educationally backward classes.

**Article 16 - Equality in Public Employment:** Exceptions: Art 16(3) residence requirement, Art 16(4) reservation, Art 16(4A) consequential seniority.

**Article 17 - Abolition of Untouchability:** Absolute right. Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955, SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989.

**Article 18 - Abolition of Titles:** Prohibits State from conferring titles. Exceptions: Military and academic distinctions.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) - 50% cap on reservation
- Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018) - Section 377 struck down
- Joseph Shine v. Union of India (2018) - Adultery decriminalized

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Distinguish between 'equality before law' and 'equal protection of laws' with case law.
summary: Articles 14-18 guarantee Right to Equality. Article 14 has twin test of reasonable classification. Articles 15-18 prohibit discrimination, abolish untouchability and titles.

title: Fundamental Rights - Articles 19 to 22
subject: Constitutional Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Article 19 - Six Freedoms:** Available only to citizens.
(1) Speech and expression - Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950)
(2) Assemble peaceably and without arms
(3) Form associations/unions/cooperatives
(4) Move freely throughout India
(5) Reside and settle in any part
(6) Practice any profession, trade, or business

**Article 20 - Protection in Respect of Conviction:** (1) Ex post facto law, (2) Double jeopardy, (3) Self-incrimination.

**Article 21 - Right to Life:** Most dynamic right. 'Procedure established by law' interpreted as 'due process' in Maneka Gandhi (1978).

**Expanded Rights under Art 21:** Right to privacy (Puttaswamy 2017), livelihood (Olga Tellis), clean environment (M.C. Mehta), health, education (21A), speedy trial, legal aid, sleep, die with dignity (Common Cause 2018).

**Article 22 - Protection Against Arrest:** (1) Right to be informed of grounds, (2) Right to consult lawyer, (3) Production before magistrate within 24 hours. Preventive detention under Art 22(4)-(7).

**Landmark Cases:**
- Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) AIR 1978 SC 597
- K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) 10 SCC 1
- Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) - Section 66A struck down
- D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997) - Arrest guidelines

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** 'Article 21 is the heart of Fundamental Rights.' Discuss with landmark judgments.
summary: Articles 19-22 cover six freedoms, protection against conviction, right to life (expanded by judiciary), and protection against arrest. Golden Triangle: Arts 14, 19, 21 interconnected.

title: Directive Principles & Fundamental Duties
subject: Constitutional Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**DPSP (Part IV, Articles 36-51):** Non-justiciable (Art 37) but fundamental in governance.

**Classification:**
1. Socialistic (Arts 38-43A): Welfare state, equal pay, right to work, living wage
2. Gandhian (Arts 40, 43, 46-48): Village panchayats, SC/ST promotion, agriculture
3. Liberal-Intellectual (Arts 44, 45, 48A, 49-51): UCC, education, environment, monuments, international peace

**Conflict with FR:** Champakam Dorairajan (1951) - FR prevail. Minerva Mills (1980) - Harmonious construction. 42nd Amendment gave DPSP primacy over Arts 14, 19.

**Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Art 51A):** Added by 42nd Amendment (1976) on Swaran Singh Committee recommendation. 11 duties. Not enforceable but can be used for interpretation.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) - Harmony between FR and DPSP
- Unnikrishnan v. State of AP (1993) - Right to education under Art 21
- Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka (1992) - Capitation fees

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Discuss the relationship between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles of State Policy.
summary: DPSP (Part IV) are non-justiciable but fundamental in governance. Classified into Socialistic, Gandhian, and Liberal-Intellectual. Fundamental Duties (Art 51A) added by 42nd Amendment.

title: Writ Jurisdiction & Constitutional Remedies
subject: Constitutional Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Article 32 - Supreme Court Writ Jurisdiction:** Dr. Ambedkar called it 'the heart and soul of the Constitution'. Five writs:

1. **Habeas Corpus** - 'Produce the body'. Against unlawful detention. Can be filed by any person on behalf of detainee. ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (1976) - suspended during Emergency.

2. **Mandamus** - 'We command'. Commands public authority to perform statutory duty. Cannot be against private individual, President, or Governor.

3. **Prohibition** - Forbids lower court/tribunal from exceeding jurisdiction. Issued during proceedings.

4. **Certiorari** - Quashes order of inferior court/tribunal. Issued after order is passed. Grounds: lack of jurisdiction, error of law apparent on face of record, violation of natural justice.

5. **Quo Warranto** - 'By what authority'. Challenges unlawful occupation of public office.

**Article 226 - High Court Writ Jurisdiction:** Wider than Art 32. Can be issued for FRs and 'for any other purpose'.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950) - Art 32 is itself a FR
- L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India (1997) - HC power under Art 226 is part of basic structure
- Nilabati Behera v. State of Orissa (1993) - Compensation for rights violation

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** Compare the writ jurisdiction of Supreme Court under Article 32 and High Courts under Article 226.
summary: Article 32 (SC) and Article 226 (HC) grant writ jurisdiction. Five writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto. Art 226 is wider in scope.

title: Jurisdiction of Civil Courts & Res Judicata
subject: Civil Procedure
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Section 9 CPC - Jurisdiction of Civil Courts:** Courts have jurisdiction to try all suits of civil nature EXCEPT: (a) expressly barred, (b) impliedly barred. Burden of proof is on party ousting jurisdiction.

**Res Judicata (Section 11):** No court shall try any suit or issue which has been directly and substantially in issue in a former suit between same parties, under same title, in a court competent to try such subsequent suit, and has been heard and finally decided.

**Conditions of Res Judicata:**
1. Matter directly and substantially in issue
2. Former suit between same parties or parties under whom they claim
3. Litigating under same title
4. Court competent to try subsequent suit
5. Matter heard and finally decided

**Constructive Res Judicata:** Explanation IV - Matter which might and ought to have been made ground of attack/defence is deemed to have been constructively in issue.

**Res Sub Judice (Section 10):** Stay of suit when matter is directly and substantially in issue in another pending suit.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Daryao v. State of UP (1961) - Res judicata applies to writ petitions
- Satyadhyan Ghosal v. Deorajin Debi (1960) - Principles of res judicata
- Sheodan Singh v. Daryao Kunwar (1966) - Competency of court

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Explain the doctrine of res judicata under Section 11 CPC with its essential conditions.
summary: Section 9 CPC grants civil courts jurisdiction over all civil suits except expressly/impliedly barred. Section 11 embodies res judicata - matter finally decided cannot be re-litigated.

title: Pleadings - Plaint and Written Statement
subject: Civil Procedure
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Order VI - Pleadings:** Pleadings means plaint or written statement. Purpose: to narrow down issues and give notice to opposite party.

**Fundamental Rules (Order VI Rule 2):** Pleadings must state material facts only, not evidence. Must be concise. Must be divided into paragraphs. Dates, sums, numbers in figures.

**Plaint (Order VII):** Must contain: name of court, name/address of parties, facts constituting cause of action, jurisdiction, relief claimed, valuation, verification (Order VI Rule 15).

**Rejection of Plaint (Order VII Rule 11):** (a) No cause of action, (b) undervalued and not corrected, (c) insufficiently stamped, (d) barred by law.

**Written Statement (Order VIII):** Must specifically deny allegations. Evasive denial = admission. Set-off and counter-claim. New grounds must be raised with leave of court.

**Amendment of Pleadings (Order VI Rule 17):** Court may allow amendment at any stage for determining real questions in controversy. After trial begins, only if court could not have discovered with due diligence.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Kishan Das v. Nathu Ram (1990) - Material facts vs evidence
- Rajesh Kumar Aggarwal v. K.K. Mohindroo (2017) - Amendment principles
- Salem Advocate Bar Association v. Union of India (2005) - Case management

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** When can a plaint be rejected under Order VII Rule 11 CPC? Discuss with case law.
summary: Pleadings (Order VI) must state material facts, not evidence. Plaint (Order VII) initiates suit. Written Statement (Order VIII) is defence. Order VII Rule 11 provides for rejection of plaint.

title: Interim Orders - Injunctions & Temporary Relief
subject: Civil Procedure
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Order 39 Rules 1-2 - Temporary Injunctions:** Court may grant temporary injunction to prevent waste, damage, alienation of property, or wrongful dispossession.

**Three Tests (American Cyanamid Co. v. Ethicon Ltd. - adopted in India):**
1. Prima facie case
2. Balance of convenience
3. Irreparable injury

**Order 39 Rule 3 - Notice:** Injunction not to be granted without notice to opposite party unless court records reasons for urgency.

**Order 39 Rule 4 - Discharge/Variation:** Court may discharge, vary, or set aside injunction on application of any party.

**Order 39 Rule 5 - Injunction to Corporation:** Against officers of corporation.

**Order 39 Rule 6 - Security:** Court may require security for compliance.

**Order 39 Rule 7 - Detention/Preservation:** Court may appoint commissioner for detention, preservation, inspection of property.

**Order 39 Rule 9 - Ex Parte Injunction:** Can be granted in urgent cases but must be decided within 30 days (2018 Amendment).

**Landmark Cases:**
- Dalpat Kumar v. Prahlad Singh (1993) - Three tests for injunction
- Morgan Stanley v. Kartik Das (2021) - Ex parte injunction principles
- Siliguri Municipality v. Nanda Rani (2022) - Mandatory vs prohibitory injunction

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** What are the conditions for granting temporary injunction under Order 39 CPC?
summary: Order 39 CPC provides for temporary injunctions. Three tests: prima facie case, balance of convenience, irreparable injury. Ex parte injunctions must be decided within 30 days.

title: Appeals, Revisions & Reviews
subject: Civil Procedure
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**First Appeal (Section 96):** Appeal from original decree. Both questions of law and fact. Lies to District Court or High Court.

**Second Appeal (Section 100):** Only on substantial question of law. Must be formulated at time of admission. Cannot be entertained unless court is satisfied it involves substantial question of law.

**Review (Section 114 + Order 47):** Review by same court and same judge. Grounds: (a) discovery of new evidence, (b) error apparent on face of record, (c) sufficient reason. Must be filed within 30 days.

**Revision (Section 115):** High Court may revise any case decided by subordinate court where: (a) no appeal lies, (b) jurisdiction exercised illegally or with material irregularity. 2002 Amendment restricted revision to cases where order would finally dispose of suit.

**Letters Patent Appeal:** Intra-court appeal under Letters Patent of High Court.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Sir Chunilal v. Mehta & Sons (1962) - Substantial question of law test
- Ganga Bai v. Ganesh Ram (1881) - Right of appeal is substantive
- Shyam Kumar v. Addl. District Judge (2020) - Scope of Section 115 revision

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Distinguish between review and revision under CPC with reference to their scope and limitations.
summary: Section 96: First appeal on law and fact. Section 100: Second appeal only on substantial question of law. Section 114: Review by same judge. Section 115: Revision by High Court.

title: Execution of Decrees & Orders
subject: Civil Procedure
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Section 2(2) - Decree:** Formal expression of adjudication conclusively determining rights of parties. Preliminary decree declares rights; final decree completely disposes of suit.

**Section 51 - Modes of Execution:** (a) Delivery of property, (b) Attachment and sale, (c) Arrest and detention, (d) Appointment of receiver, (e) Any other manner.

**Section 58 - Detention in Civil Prison:** Judgment-debtor may be detained if decree is for sum exceeding Rs. 2,000 and debtor has means to pay but refuses.

**Section 60 - Property Liable to Attachment:** All saleable property belonging to judgment-debtor. Exemptions: wearing apparel, cooking utensils, tools of artisan, necessary books, agricultural implements, wages, pension.

**Order 21 - Execution Procedure:** Application for execution (Order 21 Rule 11). Notice to show cause (Rule 37). Attachment before judgment (Order 38).

**Landmark Cases:**
- Ghanshyam Das v. Anant Kumar (1957) - Executable decree
- Sundar Singh v. Ram Lal (2019) - Attachment of property
- U.P. Jal Nigam v. Kalra Properties (1996) - Execution proceedings

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** What properties are exempt from attachment and sale in execution of a decree under Section 60 CPC?
summary: Execution of decrees under Sections 51-74 and Order 21 CPC. Modes include delivery of property, attachment and sale, arrest, and receivership. Section 60 lists exempt properties.

title: Relevancy of Facts & Res Gestae
subject: Evidence Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Section 5 (BSA 2023 / IEA 1872):** Evidence may be given of facts in issue and relevant facts. Facts in issue are those from which legal right/liability arises.

**Res Gestae (Section 6):** Facts forming part of same transaction are relevant. Spontaneous statements made during or immediately after an event are admissible.

**Conditions for Res Gestae:**
1. Statement must be contemporaneous with the act
2. Must relate to the same transaction
3. Must be spontaneous, not after deliberation
4. No time for fabrication

**Section 7 - Facts Occasion, Cause, Effect:** Facts which are occasion, cause, or effect of facts in issue are relevant.

**Section 8 - Motive, Preparation, Conduct:** Facts showing motive, preparation, and previous/subsequent conduct are relevant.

**Section 9 - Explanatory Facts:** Facts necessary to explain or introduce relevant facts.

**Section 10 - Conspiracy:** Statements by conspirators in reference to common intention are relevant.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Rattan Singh v. State of HP (1997) - Res gestae principles
- Gentela Vijayavardhan Rao v. State of AP (1996) - Spontaneity test
- State of Punjab v. Baldev Singh (1999) - Section 6 application

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Explain the doctrine of res gestae under Section 6 of the Evidence Act with illustrations.
summary: Section 5: Evidence of facts in issue and relevant facts. Section 6 (Res Gestae): Facts forming part of same transaction are relevant. Sections 7-10 cover occasion, motive, explanatory facts, and conspiracy.

title: Admissions, Confessions & Dying Declaration
subject: Evidence Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Admissions (Sections 17-23 BSA/IEA):** Statement suggesting inference as to fact in issue. Civil cases: binding on maker. Criminal cases: not conclusive but relevant.

**Confessions (Sections 24-30 BSA/IEA):**
- Section 24: Confession caused by inducement, threat, or promise is irrelevant
- Section 25: Confession to police officer is irrelevant
- Section 26: Confession in immediate presence of Magistrate is admissible
- Section 27: Discovery of fact - information leading to discovery is admissible

**Dying Declaration (Section 32(1)):** Statement by person as to cause of death or circumstances of transaction resulting in death. No oath required. No cross-examination.

**Tests for Dying Declaration:**
1. Deceased was in fit mental state
2. Statement was voluntary and truthful
3. Corroboration not mandatory but desirable
4. Multiple dying declarations must be consistent

**Landmark Cases:**
- Paniben v. State of Gujarat (1992) - Dying declaration principles
- Laxman v. State of Maharashtra (2002) - No particular form required
- State of UP v. Ram Sagar Yadav (1985) - Confession to police

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** When is a confession to a police officer admissible? Discuss Section 27 of the Evidence Act.
summary: Admissions (Sections 17-23) are relevant but not conclusive. Confessions to police inadmissible (Section 25) except discovery (Section 27). Dying declaration (Section 32) is admissible without oath.

title: Burden of Proof & Estoppel
subject: Evidence Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Burden of Proof (Sections 101-114 BSA/IEA):**
- Section 101: Whoever asserts must prove
- Section 102: Burden lies on party who would fail if no evidence given
- Section 103: Burden of proving particular fact
- Section 104: Burden of proving facts showing admissibility
- Section 105: Burden of proving exception (accused)
- Section 106: Burden of proving facts within special knowledge

**Presumptions (Sections 114-114B):**
- May presume (discretionary)
- Shall presume (rebuttable)
- Conclusive proof (irrebuttable)

**Estoppel (Section 115):** When one person intentionally causes another to believe a thing to be true and act upon it, neither party can deny it.

**Kinds of Estoppel:** By record, by deed, by conduct, promissory estoppel.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Sudhir Kumar Singh (1976) - Burden of proof
- Motilal Padampat v. State of UP (1979) - Promissory estoppel
- Dattaraj Nathu Thakur v. State of Maharashtra (1994) - Section 106

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** Explain the doctrine of burden of proof. When does the burden shift to the accused?
summary: Burden of proof lies on party asserting a fact (Section 101). Section 106: facts within special knowledge. Estoppel (Section 115) prevents denial of representation acted upon.

title: Expert Opinion & Documentary Evidence
subject: Evidence Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Expert Opinion (Sections 45-51 BSA/IEA):**
- Section 45: Opinion of experts on science, art, foreign law, identity of handwriting, finger impressions
- Section 46: Facts supporting expert opinion
- Section 47: Opinion on handwriting when person is acquainted
- Section 48: Opinion on existence of right/custom
- Section 49: Opinion on usages/tenets

**Documentary Evidence (Sections 61-90 BSA/IEA):**
- Primary evidence: Original document itself
- Secondary evidence: Copies, oral account of contents
- Section 65: Cases where secondary evidence may be given
- Section 67: Proof of signature and handwriting
- Section 68: Proof of execution of document required by law to be attested

**Electronic Evidence (Section 65B):** Certificate required for admissibility of electronic records.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Ram Babu Misra (1980) - Expert opinion weight
- Arjun Panditrao v. Kailash Kushanrao (2020) - Section 65B certificate mandatory
- Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974) - Handwriting expert

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Discuss the evidentiary value of expert opinion under Section 45 of the Evidence Act.
summary: Expert opinion (Sections 45-51) covers science, art, handwriting, fingerprints. Documentary evidence: primary vs secondary. Section 65B certificate mandatory for electronic evidence.

title: Witnesses & Examination of Witnesses
subject: Evidence Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Competency of Witnesses (Sections 118-134 BSA/IEA):**
- Section 118: All persons competent unless prevented by tender age, extreme old age, disease, or similar cause
- Accomplice: Competent but corroboration desirable
- Child witness: Must understand questions and give rational answers
- Interested witness: Testimony not rejected solely on ground of interest

**Examination of Witnesses (Sections 135-166 BSA/IEA):**
- Examination-in-chief: By party calling witness
- Cross-examination: By adverse party - tests credibility
- Re-examination: By party calling witness - clarifies matters from cross-examination
- Leading questions: Not allowed in chief, allowed in cross-examination

**Impeaching Credit (Section 155):** By evidence of persons showing witness is unworthy of credit, by proof of contradictory statements, by evidence of bad character.

**Hostile Witness (Section 154):** Court may permit cross-examination by party calling witness.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of Kerala v. Babu (2022) - Child witness reliability
- Jagga Singh v. State of Punjab (1996) - Hostile witness
- Zahira Habibullah v. State of Gujarat (2004) - Witness protection

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** When can a witness be declared hostile? What is the evidentiary value of hostile witness testimony?
summary: All persons are competent witnesses (Section 118). Examination: chief, cross, re-examination. Leading questions allowed only in cross-examination. Hostile witness under Section 154.

title: General Principles of Criminal Liability
subject: Criminal Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Mens Rea + Actus Reus:** Fundamental maxim - 'Actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea' (An act does not make a person guilty unless the mind is also guilty).

**Stages of Crime:**
1. Intention - not punishable unless specifically provided
2. Preparation - generally not punishable (exception: waging war, dacoity)
3. Attempt - punishable (Section 511 IPC / corresponding BNS provision)
4. Commission - full offence

**Common Intention (Section 34 IPC/BNS):** Criminal act done by several persons in furtherance of common intention of all. Each person liable as if act done by him alone.

**Common Object (Section 149 IPC/BNS):** Unlawful assembly of 5+ persons. Every member liable for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

**Abetment (Sections 107-120B IPC/BNS):** Instigation, conspiracy, intentional aid. Abetment of offence is punishable even if offence not committed.

**Criminal Conspiracy (Section 120A-B):** Agreement between 2+ persons to do illegal act. Punishable even if conspiracy not carried out.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Mahbub Shah v. Emperor (1945) - Common intention requires prior meeting of minds
- Barendra Kumar Ghosh v. Emperor (1925) - Section 34 liability
- State of MP v. Ram Singh (2020) - Common object vs common intention

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Distinguish between common intention (Section 34) and common object (Section 149) with case law.
summary: Criminal liability requires mens rea and actus reus. Stages: intention, preparation, attempt, commission. Common intention (Section 34) vs common object (Section 149). Abetment and conspiracy.

title: General Exceptions - Defences in Criminal Law
subject: Criminal Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**General Exceptions (Sections 76-106 IPC / corresponding BNS):**

**Mistake of Fact (Section 76/79):** Act done by person bound by law, or believing in good faith justified by law, is not offence.

**Accident (Section 80):** Nothing is offence which is done by accident or misfortune without criminal intention, in doing lawful act by lawful means.

**Infancy (Sections 82-83):** Under 7 years: absolute immunity. 7-12 years: immunity if insufficient maturity.

**Insanity (Section 84):** Act by person of unsound mind - incapable of knowing nature of act or that it is wrong/contrary to law.

**Intoxication (Sections 85-86):** Involuntary intoxication: defence. Voluntary: no defence.

**Private Defence (Sections 96-106):** Right against unlawful aggression. Extends to causing death for: murder, grievous hurt, rape, kidnapping, acid attack. Must be proportionate.

**Necessity (Section 81):** Act likely to cause harm but done without criminal intention to prevent other harm.

**Landmark Cases:**
- K.M. Nanavati v. State of Bombay (1962) - Grave and sudden provocation
- State of UP v. Ram Swarup (1964) - Private defence
- Dahyabhai Chhaganbhai v. State of Gujarat (1964) - Insanity defence

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Explain the right of private defence. When does it extend to causing death?
summary: General exceptions provide defences: mistake of fact, accident, infancy, insanity, intoxication, private defence, necessity. Burden of proof on accused (preponderance of probability).

title: Offences Against Human Body
subject: Criminal Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Culpable Homicide (Section 299 IPC / BNS equivalent):** Death caused by act with intention of causing death, or intention of causing bodily injury likely to cause death, or knowledge that act is likely to cause death.

**Murder (Section 300 IPC / BNS equivalent):** Culpable homicide is murder if: (1) Intention to cause death, (2) Intention to cause bodily injury known to be likely to cause death, (3) Intention to cause bodily injury sufficient in ordinary course to cause death, (4) Knowledge that act is so imminently dangerous that it must cause death.

**Exceptions to Murder (Section 300):**
1. Grave and sudden provocation
2. Private defence exceeding limit
3. Public servant exceeding powers
4. Sudden fight
5. Consent (for person above 18)

**Hurt (Section 319-320):** Simple hurt vs grievous hurt (emasculation, loss of eye/ear, fracture, disfiguration, etc.).

**Wrongful Restraint/Confinement (Section 339-344):** Obstructing passage vs depriving liberty.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Virsa Singh v. State of Punjab (1958) - Third clause of Section 300
- K.M. Nanavati v. State of Bombay (1962) - Provocation
- State of Andhra Pradesh v. Rayavarapu Punnayya (1976) - Culpable homicide vs murder

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** Distinguish between culpable homicide and murder. Explain the exceptions to murder.
summary: Culpable homicide (Section 299) becomes murder (Section 300) when intention/knowledge is higher. Five exceptions reduce murder to culpable homicide. Grievous hurt has 8 specific categories.

title: Offences Against Property
subject: Criminal Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Theft (Section 378 IPC / BNS equivalent):** Dishonest taking of movable property out of possession of another without consent. Moving property in order to such taking.

**Extortion (Section 383):** Intentionally putting person in fear of injury and dishonestly inducing delivery of property.

**Robbery (Section 390):** Theft + violence/threat at time of committing theft. Extortion + immediate threat of death/hurt.

**Dacoity (Section 391):** Robbery committed by 5 or more persons.

**Criminal Misappropriation (Section 403):** Dishonest misappropriation of movable property.

**Criminal Breach of Trust (Section 405):** Entrusted property dishonestly misappropriated.

**Cheating (Section 415):** Deception inducing delivery of property or consent to retention.

**Mischief (Section 425):** Intentional destruction/diminution of value of property.

**Criminal Trespass (Section 441):** Entry into property in possession of another with intent to commit offence/intimidate/insult/annoy.

**Landmark Cases:**
- K.N. Mehra v. State of Rajasthan (1957) - Theft definition
- State of Maharashtra v. Vishwanath (1979) - Robbery vs extortion
- Ramakant Rai v. Madan Rai (2003) - Criminal breach of trust

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Distinguish between theft, extortion, robbery, and dacoity with illustrations.
summary: Offences against property: theft (dishonest taking), extortion (fear-induced delivery), robbery (theft + violence), dacoity (robbery by 5+), criminal breach of trust, cheating, mischief, trespass.

title: Offences Against Women & State
subject: Criminal Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Rape (Section 375 IPC / BNS equivalent):** Sexual intercourse under 7 circumstances: against will, without consent, consent by fear, consent under misconception, under 18 years, unable to understand, without consent. Exception: marital rape (wife above 18).

**2013 Amendment (Nirbhaya Case):** Expanded definition, enhanced punishment, new offences: acid attack, stalking, voyeurism, disrobing.

**Dowry Death (Section 304B):** Death of woman within 7 years of marriage under unnatural circumstances, subjected to cruelty/harassment for dowry. Presumption of guilt.

**Cruelty (Section 498A):** Husband/relative subjecting woman to cruelty for dowry or driving to suicide.

**Offences Against State (Sections 121-130):** Waging war against Government (Section 121), sedition (Section 124A - struck down in SG Vomboti v. Union of India 2022), promoting enmity between groups.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Mukesh & Anr v. State for NCT of Delhi (2017) - Nirbhaya case
- Independent Thought v. Union of India (2017) - Marital rape exception
- SG Vomboti v. Union of India (2022) - Sedition struck down
- Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014) - Section 498A arrest guidelines

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Discuss the legal framework for protection of women against domestic violence and dowry-related offences.
summary: Rape (Section 375) expanded by 2013 amendment. Dowry death (Section 304B) has presumption of guilt. Sedition (Section 124A) struck down in 2022. Special protections for women.

title: Formation of Contract - Offer & Acceptance
subject: Contract Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Offer/Proposal (Section 2(a)):** When one person signifies to another his willingness to do/abstain from doing anything, with a view to obtaining assent.

**Kinds of Offer:** Specific (to particular person), General (to world at large - Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co.), Express, Implied, Cross offers.

**Acceptance (Section 2(b)):** When person to whom proposal is made signifies assent thereto.

**Rules of Valid Acceptance:**
1. Must be absolute and unqualified
2. Must be communicated
3. Must be in prescribed mode
4. Must be by person to whom offer is made
5. Must be given within reasonable time

**Communication (Sections 3-5):** Proposal complete when comes to knowledge of offeree. Acceptance complete against proposer when put in transmission (postal rule - Adams v. Lindsell).

**Revocation:** Proposal revoked before acceptance is complete against proposer. Acceptance revoked before letter reaches proposer.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. (1893) - General offer
- Lalman Shukla v. Gauri Dutt (1913) - Acceptance must be with knowledge of offer
- Bhagwandas v. Girdharilal (1966) - Telephonic acceptance

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Explain the rules of valid acceptance. When is communication of acceptance complete?
summary: Offer (Section 2(a)) must be communicated and definite. Acceptance (Section 2(b)) must be absolute, communicated, and in prescribed mode. Postal rule applies to acceptance by post.

title: Consideration & Privity of Contract
subject: Contract Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Consideration (Section 2(d)):** When at desire of promisor, promisee or any other person has done/abstained from doing, does/abstains from doing, or promises to do/abstain from doing something.

**Rules:**
1. Must move at desire of promisor (Durga Prasad v. Baldeo)
2. May move from promisee or any other person (Chinnaya v. Ramaya)
3. May be past, present, or future
4. Need not be adequate but must be real
5. Must be lawful (Section 23)

**Exceptions to 'No Consideration, No Contract' (Section 25):**
1. Natural love and affection (written, registered, near relatives)
2. Compensation for past voluntary service
3. Promise to pay time-barred debt
4. Completed gift
5. Agency (Section 185)
6. Gratuitous bailment (Section 148)

**Privity of Contract:** Only parties to contract can sue. Exceptions: trust/marriage settlement, family arrangements, collateral contracts, covenants running with land.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Chinnaya v. Ramaya (1882) - Consideration from third party
- Mohiri Bibi v. Dharmodas Ghose (1903) - Minor's agreement void
- Dunlop v. Selfridge (1915) - Privity of contract

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** 'Consideration need not be adequate but must be real.' Explain with exceptions under Section 25.
summary: Consideration (Section 2(d)) is quid pro quo. May move from any person. Section 25 exceptions: love/affection, past service, time-barred debt, gift, agency, bailment. Privity rule with exceptions.

title: Free Consent - Coercion, Fraud & Misrepresentation
subject: Contract Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Free Consent (Section 14):** Consent not caused by coercion, undue influence, fraud, misrepresentation, or mistake.

**Coercion (Section 15):** Committing/threatening act forbidden by IPC, or unlawfully detaining property. Key: Chikkam Ammiraju v. Chikkam Seshamma - threat to commit suicide = coercion.

**Undue Influence (Section 16):** One party dominates will of another. Presumed in: parent-child, guardian-ward, trustee-beneficiary, spiritual guru-disciple.

**Fraud (Section 17):** False suggestion of fact, active concealment, promise without intention, deceptive act, or any act declared fraudulent.

**Misrepresentation (Section 18):** Innocent false statement, breach of duty without intent to deceive, or inducing mistake about subject matter.

**Mistake (Sections 20-22):** Mistake of both parties as to fact essential to agreement = void. Mistake of law = not void. Unilateral mistake = not voidable except for identity.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Chikkam Ammiraju v. Chikkam Seshamma (1917) - Suicide threat = coercion
- Derry v. Peek (1889) - Fraud requires knowledge of falsity
- Bisset v. Wilkinson (1927) - Opinion vs fact

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Distinguish between fraud and misrepresentation. What are the effects on contract validity?
summary: Free consent requires absence of coercion, undue influence, fraud, misrepresentation, and mistake. Coercion is criminal; undue influence is equitable. Fraud is intentional; misrepresentation is innocent.

title: Void & Voidable Agreements
subject: Contract Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Void Agreements (Sections 24-30):**
1. Agreement with unlawful object/consideration (Section 23)
2. Agreement in restraint of marriage (Section 26)
3. Agreement in restraint of trade (Section 27) - Exception: sale of goodwill
4. Agreement in restraint of legal proceedings (Section 28)
5. Uncertain agreement (Section 29)
6. Wagering agreement (Section 30)

**Voidable Agreements:** Consent obtained by coercion, fraud, misrepresentation, or undue influence. Voidable at option of aggrieved party.

**Contingent Contracts (Sections 31-36):** Enforcement depends on happening/non-happening of uncertain future event.

**Quasi-Contracts (Sections 68-72):**
- Section 68: Necessaries supplied to incapable person
- Section 69: Payment by interested person
- Section 70: Obligation of person enjoying benefit of non-gratuitous act
- Section 71: Finder of goods
- Section 72: Money paid by mistake/coercion

**Landmark Cases:**
- Mohiri Bibi v. Dharmodas Ghose (1903) - Minor's agreement void ab initio
- Gherulal Parakh v. Mahadeodas Maiya (1959) - Wagering agreements
- State of Orissa v. Bhagaban (1963) - Quasi-contract

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** Explain quasi-contracts under the Indian Contract Act. How do they differ from true contracts?
summary: Void agreements: unlawful object, restraint of marriage/trade/legal proceedings, uncertain, wagering. Voidable: consent vitiated. Quasi-contracts (Sections 68-72) based on equity, not agreement.

title: Discharge & Remedies for Breach of Contract
subject: Contract Law
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Modes of Discharge:**
1. By performance (actual or attempted/tender)
2. By mutual agreement (novation, alteration, rescission, remission, accord and satisfaction)
3. By impossibility/frustration (Section 56)
4. By lapse of time (Limitation Act)
5. By operation of law (death, merger, insolvency)
6. By breach of contract

**Doctrine of Frustration (Section 56):** Contract becomes void when act becomes impossible or unlawful. Applies to subsequent impossibility, not initial.

**Remedies for Breach:**
1. Damages (Section 73) - Compensatory, not punitive. Rule in Hadley v. Baxendale: damages arising naturally or in contemplation of parties.
2. Quantum meruit - As much as earned
3. Specific performance (Specific Relief Act)
4. Injunction
5. Liquidated damages vs penalty (Section 74)

**Landmark Cases:**
- Satyabrata Ghose v. Mugneeram Bangur (1954) - Doctrine of frustration
- Hadley v. Baxendale (1854) - Rule of remoteness of damages
- Fateh Chand v. Balkishan Das (1963) - Liquidated damages vs penalty

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Explain the doctrine of frustration under Section 56. What remedies are available for breach of contract?
summary: Discharge: performance, mutual agreement, frustration, lapse of time, operation of law, breach. Remedies: damages (Hadley v. Baxendale rule), quantum meruit, specific performance, injunction.

title: Nature & Sources of Law
subject: Jurisprudence
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Jurisprudence:** 'Eye of law' - study of fundamental principles of law. Etymology: 'Juris' (law) + 'Prudentia' (knowledge/skill).

**Definitions:** Austin - 'Philosophy of positive law'. Salmond - 'Science of first principles of civil law'. Holland - 'Formal science of positive law'.

**Sources of Law:**
1. Custom - Most ancient source. Requirements: antiquity, continuity, reasonableness, conformity with statute, peaceable enjoyment
2. Legislation - Supreme source in modern state. Supreme/Parliamentary and Subordinate/Delegated
3. Precedent - Judge-made law. Stare decisis - let the decision stand
4. Professional literature - Writings of jurists
5. Convention - Constitutional conventions

**Kinds of Law:** Natural law, positive law, private law, public law, civil law, criminal law, substantive law, procedural law.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Renusagar Power Co. (1988) - Custom as source of law
- Bengal Immunity Co. v. State of Bihar (1955) - Precedent and stare decisis

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Discuss the various sources of law. Which is the most important source in modern legal systems?
summary: Jurisprudence studies fundamental principles of law. Sources: custom, legislation, precedent, professional literature, convention. Legislation is supreme in modern legal systems.

title: Schools of Jurisprudence - Analytical & Historical
subject: Jurisprudence
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Analytical School (Austin):**
- Law is command of sovereign backed by sanction
- Sovereign: determinate human superior receiving habitual obedience
- Separation of law as it IS from law as it OUGHT to be
- Criticism: Ignores customary law, international law, constitutional law

**Historical School (Savigny, Maine):**
- Savigny: Law is found, not made. Volksgeist (spirit of people). Law grows like language
- Maine: Status to Contract - evolution of law from family-based to individual-based society
- Stages: (1) Commands of king, (2) Customary law, (3) Administration of justice

**Comparison:** Austin focuses on sovereign command; Savigny on organic growth. Austin is deductive; Savigny is inductive.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Madhav Rao Scindia v. Union of India (1993) - Customary law recognition
- Sarla Mudgal v. Union of India (1995) - Uniform Civil Code debate

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** Compare Austin's analytical school with Savigny's historical school of jurisprudence.
summary: Analytical School (Austin): law = sovereign command + sanction. Historical School (Savigny): law = Volksgeist, organic growth. Maine: evolution from status to contract.

title: Sociological & Philosophical Schools
subject: Jurisprudence
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Sociological School:**
- Ihering: Social purpose of law. Law as means to social ends
- Duguit: Social solidarity. Law based on social interdependence
- Roscoe Pound: Social engineering. Law as tool to balance competing interests. Jural postulates of civilization. Interests: individual, public, social

**Philosophical School (Natural Law):**
- Ancient: Aristotle - natural justice vs legal justice
- Medieval: Aquinas - divine law, eternal law, natural law, human law
- Modern: Grotius, Locke, Rousseau - Social contract theory
- Revival: Post-WWII, Fuller - inner morality of law (8 principles)

**Realist School:**
- American Realism: Holmes - 'Life of law has not been logic but experience'
- Scandinavian Realism: Hagerstrom - law as facts, not norms

**Landmark Cases:**
- Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) - Natural law influence
- K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) - Dignity as constitutional value

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Explain Roscoe Pound's theory of social engineering and jural postulates.
summary: Sociological School: Pound's social engineering, balancing interests. Philosophical School: Natural law from ancient to modern. Realist School: law as experience, not logic.

title: Rights, Duties & Liability
subject: Jurisprudence
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Legal Rights (Salmond):** Interest recognized and protected by law. Elements: (1) Person of inherence, (2) Person of incidence, (3) Subject matter, (4) Object, (5) Title.

**Kinds of Rights:** Perfect/imperfect, positive/negative, real/personal, proprietary/personal, in rem/in personam.

**Hohfeld's Analysis:** Right-Duty, Privilege-No Right, Power-Liability, Immunity-Disability.

**Legal Duties:** Correlative of rights. Positive duty (to do) vs negative duty (to abstain).

**Legal Liability:** Responsibility for consequences of act. Civil liability (compensation) vs criminal liability (punishment). Strict liability vs absolute liability.

**Ownership vs Possession:** Ownership = bundle of rights. Possession = de facto control. Types: corporeal/incorporeal, sole/joint, absolute/conditional.

**Landmark Cases:**
- M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987) - Absolute liability
- R. Rajagopal v. State of TN (1994) - Right to privacy

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** Explain Hohfeld's analytical framework of legal rights. How does it differ from Salmond's classification?
summary: Legal rights have 5 elements. Hohfeld: Right-Duty, Privilege-No Right, Power-Liability, Immunity-Disability. Ownership = rights; possession = control. Strict vs absolute liability.

title: Precedent & Stare Decisis
subject: Jurisprudence
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Precedent:** Judicial decision that serves as authority for deciding subsequent cases. Basis: stare decisis (let the decision stand).

**Kinds of Precedent:**
- Authoritative (binding) vs Persuasive (not binding)
- Original (creates new rule) vs Declaratory (applies existing rule)
- Absolute (fully binding) vs Conditional (binding unless unreasonable)

**Ratio Decidendi vs Obiter Dicta:**
- Ratio decidendi: Reason for decision - binding
- Obiter dicta: Incidental remarks - persuasive only

**Doctrine of Stare Decisis in India:**
- Supreme Court not bound by its own decisions (can overrule)
- High Court bound by Supreme Court decisions
- High Court bound by its own larger bench decisions
- Doctrine of prospective overruling (Golaknath case)

**Landmark Cases:**
- Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967) - Prospective overruling
- Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) - Overruling Golaknath
- Central Board of Dawoodi Bohra v. State of Maharashtra (2005) - When to depart from precedent

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** What is the doctrine of stare decisis? When can the Supreme Court depart from its own precedents?
summary: Precedent: binding (authoritative) and persuasive. Ratio decidendi is binding; obiter dicta is persuasive. Supreme Court can overrule its own decisions. Doctrine of prospective overruling.

title: Negligence & Duty of Care
subject: Law of Torts
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Negligence:** Breach of legal duty to take care which results in damage to another.

**Elements (Donoghue v. Stevenson):**
1. Duty of care owed to plaintiff
2. Breach of that duty
3. Damage resulting from breach
4. Reasonable foreseeability of harm

**Neighbor Principle (Lord Atkin):** You must take reasonable care to avoid acts/omissions which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure your neighbor.

**Standard of Care:** Reasonable person test. Factors: likelihood of harm, magnitude of harm, cost of precautions, utility of defendant's conduct.

**Res Ipsa Loquitur:** 'The thing speaks for itself.' Burden shifts to defendant. Conditions: (1) Event doesn't occur without negligence, (2) Defendant had exclusive control, (3) No contributory negligence.

**Defences:** Contributory negligence, volenti non fit injuria, act of God, inevitable accident, statutory authority.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Donoghue v. Stevenson (1932) - Neighbor principle
- M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987) - Absolute liability
- Jacob Mathew v. State of Punjab (2005) - Medical negligence

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Explain the elements of negligence. When does res ipsa loquitur apply?
summary: Negligence: duty of care, breach, damage, foreseeability. Donoghue v. Stevenson established neighbor principle. Res ipsa loquitur shifts burden. Defences: contributory negligence, volenti, act of God.

title: Strict & Absolute Liability
subject: Law of Torts
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Strict Liability (Rylands v. Fletcher):** Person who brings dangerous thing on land which escapes is liable for damage.

**Exceptions:**
1. Act of God (vis major)
2. Plaintiff's own default
3. Consent of plaintiff
4. Act of third party
5. Natural use of land
6. Statutory authority

**Absolute Liability (M.C. Mehta v. Union of India - Oleum Gas Leak):** No exceptions. Enterprise engaged in hazardous/inherently dangerous activity is absolutely liable for harm.

**Difference:** Strict liability has exceptions; absolute liability has none. Absolute liability applies to hazardous industries only.

**Public Liability Insurance Act 1991:** No-fault liability for hazardous substance accidents.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Rylands v. Fletcher (1868) - Strict liability rule
- M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1987) AIR 1987 SC 1086 - Absolute liability
- Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996) - Bichhri case

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** Distinguish between strict liability and absolute liability with reference to M.C. Mehta case.
summary: Strict liability (Rylands v. Fletcher) has 6 exceptions. Absolute liability (M.C. Mehta) has no exceptions and applies to hazardous industries. Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 provides no-fault compensation.

title: Defamation & Nuisance
subject: Law of Torts
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Defamation:** Publication of false statement that injures reputation.

**Libel vs Slander:**
- Libel: Permanent form (writing, print, picture) - actionable per se
- Slander: Temporary form (spoken words, gestures) - must prove special damage

**Defences to Defamation:**
1. Justification (truth) - must prove substantial truth
2. Fair comment - on matter of public interest
3. Privilege - absolute (parliamentary proceedings, judicial proceedings) and qualified (honest belief, public duty)

**Nuisance:** Unlawful interference with person's use/enjoyment of land.

**Public Nuisance:** Affects public at large. Criminal offence + civil action by AG.

**Private Nuisance:** Substantial and unreasonable interference with use/enjoyment of land.

**Remedies:** Damages, injunction, abatement.

**Landmark Cases:**
- D.P. Choudhary v. Manjulata (1997) - Defamation of student
- Radhey Shyam v. Nandu (2019) - Social media defamation
- St. Helen's Smelting Co. v. Tipping (1865) - Material damage test for nuisance

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** What are the defences available in an action for defamation? Distinguish between libel and slander.
summary: Defamation: libel (permanent, actionable per se) vs slander (temporary, requires special damage). Defences: justification, fair comment, privilege. Nuisance: public vs private.

title: Vicarious Liability & Consumer Protection
subject: Law of Torts
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Vicarious Liability:** Liability of one person for tort committed by another.

**Master-Servant:** Master liable for torts committed by servant in course of employment.

**Test:** Control test, integration test, economic reality test.

**State Liability:** State liable for torts of its servants. No sovereign immunity in non-sovereign functions (Kasturilal exception for sovereign functions).

**Partner Liability:** Partners jointly and severally liable for torts committed in ordinary course of business.

**Consumer Protection Act 2019:**
- Consumer: Buys goods/services for consideration
- Deficiency in service, defective goods, unfair trade practice
- Three-tier mechanism: District Commission, State Commission, National Commission
- Pecuniary jurisdiction: District (up to Rs. 50 lakh), State (Rs. 50 lakh - 2 crore), National (above Rs. 2 crore)
- Mediation cells, product liability, e-commerce inclusion

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of Rajasthan v. Vidyawati (1962) - State tortious liability
- Kasturilal v. State of UP (1965) - Sovereign immunity
- Nizam Institute v. Prasanth S. Dhananka (2009) - Medical negligence compensation

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** Explain vicarious liability. Is the State liable for torts committed by its servants?
summary: Vicarious liability: master-servant, partner-partner, state-servant. Consumer Protection Act 2019: three-tier mechanism, product liability, e-commerce coverage, mediation cells.

title: Trespass & Remedies in Tort
subject: Law of Torts
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Trespass to Person:**
- Battery: Intentional application of force
- Assault: Reasonable apprehension of imminent harmful/offensive contact
- False Imprisonment: Total restraint of liberty without lawful justification

**Trespass to Land:** Unauthorized entry onto land of another. Actionable per se (no damage required).

**Trespass to Goods:** Wrongful interference with goods of another.

**Remedies in Tort:**
1. Damages: Compensatory, nominal, contemptuous, aggravated, exemplary
2. Injunction: Temporary, perpetual, mandatory, prohibitory
3. Self-help: Recaption of goods, abatement of nuisance, expulsion of trespasser

**Damages Rules:**
- Damnum sine injuria: Damage without legal wrong (no remedy)
- Injuria sine damno: Legal wrong without damage (remedy available)

**Landmark Cases:**
- Ashby v. White (1703) - Injuria sine damno
- Gloucester Grammar School Case (1411) - Damnum sine injuria
- Bird v. Jones (1845) - Partial obstruction not false imprisonment

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Distinguish between damnum sine injuria and injuria sine damno with illustrations.
summary: Trespass: to person (battery, assault, false imprisonment), land, goods. Remedies: damages, injunction, self-help. Damnum sine injuria vs injuria sine damno.

title: Sale of Immovable Property
subject: Transfer of Property
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Sale (Sections 54-57):** Transfer of ownership in exchange for price paid/promised/partly paid.

**Tangible immovable property of Rs. 100+:** Registered instrument. Delivery of possession optional.

**Tangible immovable property below Rs. 100:** Registered instrument OR delivery of possession.

**Intangible property:** Registered instrument only.

**Rights & Liabilities of Seller:**
- Before completion: Right to rents/profits, duty to disclose material defects
- After completion: Right to charge, duty to give possession

**Rights & Liabilities of Buyer:**
- Before completion: Right to benefit of improvement, duty to pay
- After completion: Right to rents/profits, liability to pay public charges

**Doctrine of Lis Pendens (Section 52):** During pending suit/transfer of immovable property, any transfer affected by suit cannot affect rights of parties.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Rambaran Prosad v. Ram Mohit Hazra (1967) - Sale vs agreement to sell
- Hardwari Lal v. Chhaju Ram (1919) - Lis pendens
- S.R. Sukumar v. Sunaadara Raghuram (2012) - Lis pendens scope

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Explain the doctrine of lis pendens under Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act.
summary: Sale (Section 54): transfer of ownership for price. Requires registered instrument for property above Rs. 100. Lis pendens (Section 52): pending suit affects subsequent transfers.

title: Mortgage - Types & Rights
subject: Transfer of Property
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Mortgage (Sections 58-69):** Transfer of interest in specific immovable property for securing payment of money.

**Six Types of Mortgage:**
1. Simple mortgage: No delivery of possession, personal liability
2. Mortgage by conditional sale: Sale conditional on default
3. Usufructuary mortgage: Delivery of possession, mortgagee receives rents/profits
4. English mortgage: Absolute transfer with proviso for re-transfer
5. Equitable mortgage: Deposit of title deeds
6. Anomalous mortgage: Not fitting above categories

**Right of Redemption (Section 60):** Mortgagor's right to redeem after principal money becomes due. 'Once a mortgage, always a mortgage.'

**Clog on Redemption:** Any condition preventing redemption is void.

**Foreclosure:** Mortgagee's right to acquire absolute title when mortgagor defaults.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Khardah Company v. Raymon & Co. (1965) - Clog on redemption
- Gangadhar v. Shankar Lal (1965) - Right of redemption
- Shamsher Singh v. Suraj Narain (1996) - Anomalous mortgage

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** Explain the six types of mortgage under the Transfer of Property Act. What is the right of redemption?
summary: Six types of mortgage: simple, conditional sale, usufructuary, English, equitable, anomalous. Right of redemption (Section 60) is absolute. Clog on redemption is void.

title: Lease of Immovable Property
subject: Transfer of Property
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Lease (Sections 105-117):** Transfer of right to enjoy immovable property for a certain time or in perpetuity, for price (premium/rent).

**Essentials:** Parties (lessor/lessee), subject matter (immovable property), term, consideration.

**Lease for year-to-year or exceeding 1 year:** Registered instrument. Other leases: oral or registered.

**Rights & Duties of Lessor:** Disclosure of defects, quiet enjoyment, payment of public charges.

**Rights & Duties of Lessee:** Right to accessories, right to remove fixtures, duty to pay rent, duty to keep in repair.

**Determination of Lease (Section 111):** Expiration of term, happening of event, termination of lessor's interest, merger, express surrender, implied surrender, forfeiture, notice to quit.

**Relief Against Forfeiture (Section 114):** Court may grant relief if lessee pays rent with interest.

**Landmark Cases:**
- C. Hayavadana Rao v. Baron of Cowdor (1967) - Lease vs license
- R.M. Ayyappan v. State of Kerala (2016) - Determination of lease

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Distinguish between lease and license. How is a lease determined?
summary: Lease (Section 105): transfer of right to enjoy for term/rent. Registered for 1+ years. Determination (Section 111): expiration, surrender, forfeiture, notice. Relief against forfeiture (Section 114).

title: Gift & Exchange of Property
subject: Transfer of Property
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Gift (Sections 122-129):** Transfer of existing movable/immovable property made voluntarily and without consideration.

**Essentials:**
1. Donor must be competent
2. Donee must accept
3. Transfer must be voluntary
4. No consideration
5. Registered instrument for immovable property
6. Delivery of possession for movable property

**Revocation of Gift:** By mutual agreement, by rescission (gift obtained by fraud/misrepresentation), by donor's insolvency.

**Onerous Gift (Section 127):** Gift burdened with obligation. Donee must accept burden or reject gift entirely.

**Exchange (Sections 118-121):** Mutual transfer of ownership of one thing for another. Rights/liabilities similar to sale.

**Doctrine of Election (Section 35):** Person accepting benefit under instrument must accept whole instrument. Cannot approbate and reprobate.

**Doctrine of Feeding by Estoppel (Section 43):** Transfer by unauthorized person who later acquires interest - interest passes automatically.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Renikuntla Rajamma v. K. Narayanamma (2014) - Gift of immovable property
- Jawala Shankar v. Smt. Gauri Shankar (1970) - Doctrine of election

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** What are the essential conditions for a valid gift of immovable property? Can a gift be revoked?
summary: Gift (Sections 122-129): voluntary transfer without consideration. Requires registered instrument for immovable property. Exchange: mutual transfer. Doctrine of election and feeding by estoppel.

title: Actionable Claims & Doctrine of Part Performance
subject: Transfer of Property
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Actionable Claims (Sections 130-137):** Claim to unsecured debt or beneficial interest in movable property not in possession of claimant. Transferable by instrument in writing.

**Transfer of Actionable Claim:** By execution of instrument in writing. Notice to debtor not essential but advisable.

**Doctrine of Part Performance (Section 53A):** When transferee takes possession of property under contract and performs his part, transferor cannot enforce rights against transferee.

**Conditions:**
1. Contract in writing signed by transferor
2. For transfer of immovable property
3. Transferee takes possession or continues in possession
4. Transferee performs or is willing to perform his part
5. Transfer is not registered

**Note:** Section 53A is a shield, not a sword. Cannot be used to claim title.

**Fraudulent Transfer (Section 53):** Transfer made with intent to defeat/delay creditors is voidable at option of creditors.

**Landmark Cases:**
- S.K. Gupta v. K.P. Jain (1979) - Part performance requirements
- P.A. Shah v. D.L. Shah (2003) - Section 53A as shield

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Explain the doctrine of part performance under Section 53A. Can it be used as a sword?
summary: Actionable claims: transferable by written instrument. Part performance (Section 53A): protects transferee in possession under unregistered contract. Shield, not sword. Fraudulent transfers voidable.

title: Computation of Period of Limitation
subject: Limitation & Court Fees
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Limitation Act 1963:** Barred by limitation is a defence. Court cannot take cognizance of suit filed beyond limitation period.

**Key Sections:**
- Section 2: Definitions (applicant, application, period of limitation, suit)
- Section 3: Court bound to dismiss barred suit even if limitation not pleaded
- Section 4: Expiry on holiday - file on next working day
- Section 5: Condonation of delay - sufficient cause for appeals/applications (NOT suits)
- Section 6: Legal disability (minor, insane, idiot)
- Section 7: Disability of one of several plaintiffs
- Section 8: Special provisions for minors
- Section 9: Continuous running of period

**Periods:** Suits: 3 years (general), 12 years (immovable property), 30 years (mortgage). Appeals: 30-90 days. Applications: 30 days to 3 years.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of Nagaland v. Lipok AO (2005) - Section 3 mandatory
- Raje Gaurav Singh v. State of UP (2013) - Condonation of delay
- State of Haryana v. Chandra Mani (1997) - Section 5 not applicable to suits

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Can a court take suo motu cognizance of limitation? Discuss Section 3 of the Limitation Act.
summary: Limitation Act 1963: court must dismiss barred suits (Section 3). Section 5: condonation for appeals/applications only, not suits. Periods: 3 years general, 12 years property, 30 years mortgage.

title: Acknowledgment, Part Payment & Legal Disability
subject: Limitation & Court Fees
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Acknowledgment (Section 18):** Written acknowledgment of liability signed before expiry of limitation period. Fresh period starts from date of acknowledgment.

**Part Payment (Section 19):** Payment of interest or part of principal before expiry. Fresh period starts from date of payment. Must be in writing.

**Legal Disability (Sections 6-8):**
- Minority: Period starts from attaining majority
- Insanity: Period starts from cessation of disability
- Both: Period starts from when both disabilities cease
- Maximum extension: 3 years from cessation of disability or death

**Continuous Running (Section 9):** Once limitation starts, it runs continuously despite subsequent disability.

**Effect of Death (Sections 12-15):** Death before right to sue accrues - period starts from when representative can sue. Death after accrual - period continues.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Lachhman Das v. Ram Kishan (1970) - Acknowledgment requirements
- Punjab National Bank v. Surendra Prasad Sinha (1992) - Part payment

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** How does acknowledgment under Section 18 affect the period of limitation? What are its requirements?
summary: Acknowledgment (Section 18) and part payment (Section 19) restart limitation period. Legal disability (Sections 6-8) extends period. Once started, limitation runs continuously (Section 9).

title: Condonation of Delay & Exclusion of Time
subject: Limitation & Court Fees
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Condonation of Delay (Section 5):** Court may admit appeal/application after limitation if 'sufficient cause' shown. NOT applicable to suits.

**Sufficient Cause:** Liberal construction. Bona fide explanation. Not applicable to deliberate delay or negligence.

**Exclusion of Time (Sections 12-15):**
- Section 12: Day from which period runs excluded
- Section 13: Time spent in foreign country excluded
- Section 14: Time spent in bona fide prosecution in wrong court excluded
- Section 15: Time spent in obtaining copies of decree/judgment excluded

**Section 14 Requirements:**
1. Both courts must be of competent jurisdiction
2. Must be bona fide
3. Must be for same matter
4. Defect must be of jurisdiction or similar cause

**Landmark Cases:**
- Collector, Land Acquisition v. Mst. Katiji (1987) - Liberal approach to Section 5
- Ramesh Chand Ardawatiya v. Anil Panjwani (2003) - Section 14 conditions
- State of West Bengal v. Administrative Tribunal (2005) - Sufficient cause

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Explain the conditions for exclusion of time under Section 14 of the Limitation Act.
summary: Section 5: condonation for sufficient cause (appeals/applications only). Section 14: time in wrong court excluded if bona fide, same matter, jurisdiction defect. Liberal construction of sufficient cause.

title: Court Fees - Valuation & Ad Valorem
subject: Limitation & Court Fees
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Court Fees Act 1870 (U.P. Amendment):** Fee payable on plaint, written statement, appeal, application.

**Ad Valorem Fees:** Based on value of subject matter. Suits for money, accounts, property valuation.

**Fixed Fees:** Specific amount regardless of value. Suits for injunction, declaration, specific performance.

**Valuation for Jurisdiction vs Court Fees:** May differ. Suits Valuation Act governs jurisdiction; Court Fees Act governs fees.

**U.P. Court Fees Act 1870:**
- Section 7: Computation of fees
- Section 8: Fixed fees for specific suits
- Section 25: Refund of excess fees

**Deficiency in Court Fees:** Court may allow time to make up deficiency. If not made up, plaint rejected.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Smt. Kiran Singh v. Chaman Paswan (1954) - Valuation and jurisdiction
- Ram Narain v. State of UP (2009) - Court fees deficiency

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** How is court fee computed in suits for accounts and injunction? Distinguish between ad valorem and fixed fees.
summary: Court fees: ad valorem (based on value) vs fixed (specific amount). U.P. Court Fees Act 1870 governs computation. Deficiency may lead to rejection of plaint.

title: Special Provisions & Recent Amendments
subject: Limitation & Court Fees
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**Special Limitation Periods:**
- Section 26: Suit for compensation for wrongs - 1 year
- Section 27: Suit for possession of immovable property - 12 years
- Section 28: Extinguishment of right to property after period expires

**Article 58: Declaration** - 3 years from when right to sue accrues
**Article 59: Cancellation of instrument** - 3 years
**Article 62: Money payable by defendant** - 3 years
**Article 65: Possession of immovable property** - 12 years
**Article 113: Residuary** - 3 years

**Commercial Courts Act 2015:** Special limitation provisions for commercial disputes.

**Mediation:** Time spent in mediation excluded from limitation period.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Punjab State Civil Supplies Corp. v. New Amar Trading (2015) - Article 137 residuary
- State of Bihar v. Kameshwar Singh (1952) - Extinguishment of right

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** What is the residuary article of limitation? When does the right to property extinguish?
summary: Special periods: 1 year (wrongs), 3 years (declaration, cancellation, money), 12 years (possession), 30 years (mortgage). Article 113 is residuary (3 years). Section 28: extinguishment of right.

title: U.P. Revenue Code 2006 - Land Tenure
subject: U.P. Local Laws
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**U.P. Revenue Code 2006:** Consolidates and amends law relating to land revenue, land records, and land management in UP.

**Key Features:**
- Abolition of intermediaries (zamindari abolition)
- Bhumidhar rights: Heritable and transferable rights
- Asami rights: Tenancy rights
- Land records: Computerization and digitization
- Revenue courts: Board of Revenue, Commissioner, Collector, Revenue Court

**Bhumidhar:** Person who has paid land revenue for 6 years or is recorded as bhumidhar. Rights: transfer, mortgage, lease.

**Land Acquisition:** Reference to Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act 2013.

**Mutation:** Change in land records upon transfer, inheritance, or partition.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Chaudhary Hari Singh v. State of UP (2018) - Bhumidhar rights
- Ram Prasad v. State of UP (2020) - Mutation proceedings

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2022):** Explain the concept of Bhumidhar rights under the U.P. Revenue Code 2006.
summary: U.P. Revenue Code 2006: Bhumidhar (heritable/transferable rights), Asami (tenancy), land records digitization, revenue court hierarchy, mutation procedures.

title: U.P. Municipalities Act 1916
subject: U.P. Local Laws
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**U.P. Municipalities Act 1916:** Governs municipal administration in UP.

**Classification of Municipalities:**
- Municipal Corporation (Nagar Nigam)
- Municipal Council (Nagar Palika Parishad)
- Nagar Panchayat

**Powers & Functions:**
- Public health, sanitation, water supply
- Roads, bridges, street lighting
- Building regulation, town planning
- Taxation: property tax, water tax, octroi (abolished)

**Elections:** Direct election of members. Reservation for SC/ST, women, OBC.

**Judicial Review:** Writ jurisdiction of High Court under Article 226 for municipal actions.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Pradhan Sangh Kshetra Samiti (1995) - Municipal powers
- Aligarh Municipal Board v. Ekka Tola Singh (1990) - Taxation powers

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2021):** What are the powers and functions of Municipalities under the U.P. Municipalities Act 1916?
summary: U.P. Municipalities Act 1916: three-tier classification, public health, taxation, building regulation, elections with reservations, writ jurisdiction of High Court.

title: U.P. Panchayat Raj Act 1947
subject: U.P. Local Laws
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**U.P. Panchayat Raj Act 1947:** Three-tier Panchayati Raj system.

**Structure:**
1. Gram Panchayat (village level)
2. Kshetra Panchayat (block level)
3. Zila Panchayat (district level)

**Composition:** Elected members. Reservation for SC/ST, women (50%), OBC.

**Functions (29 subjects under 73rd Amendment):**
- Agriculture, land improvement, minor irrigation
- Rural housing, drinking water, roads
- Health, education, women and child development
- Poverty alleviation programs

**Gram Sabha:** Body of all registered voters in village area. Approves plans, budgets, audits.

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Prayag Datt (2008) - Panchayat elections
- Brij Kishore Tiwari v. State of UP (2015) - Gram Sabha powers

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2020):** Describe the three-tier Panchayati Raj system in U.P. What is the role of Gram Sabha?
summary: U.P. Panchayat Raj Act 1947: three-tier system (Gram, Kshetra, Zila Panchayat). 29 subjects under 73rd Amendment. Gram Sabha approves plans and budgets. 50% women reservation.

title: U.P. Urban Planning & Development Act 1973
subject: U.P. Local Laws
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**U.P. Urban Planning and Development Act 1973:** Regulates urban development in UP.

**Development Authorities:** Established for each development area. Functions: preparation of master plans, development plans, zoning regulations.

**Master Plan:** Long-term vision for urban development. Land use classification, transportation, housing, industrial areas.

**Development Plan:** Detailed implementation of master plan. Building by-laws, floor area ratio, setbacks.

**Land Acquisition for Development:** Under Land Acquisition Act 1894 / RFCTLARR Act 2013.

**Unauthorized Construction:** Demolition, penalty, regularization.

**Landmark Cases:**
- Friends Colony v. State of UP (2004) - Unauthorized construction
- Okhla Industrial Area v. State of UP (2012) - Master plan enforcement

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2019):** What is the function of Development Authorities under the U.P. Urban Planning and Development Act 1973?
summary: U.P. Urban Planning Act 1973: Development Authorities, master plans, development plans, zoning regulations, building by-laws, unauthorized construction enforcement.

title: U.P. Land Reforms & Tenancy Laws
subject: U.P. Local Laws
content:
**Expert Teacher Notes:**

**U.P. Land Reforms:**
- Zamindari Abolition (1950): Elimination of intermediaries
- Ceiling on land holdings: Surplus land distributed to landless
- Consolidation of holdings: Prevents fragmentation

**U.P. Tenancy Laws:**
- Protected tenancy rights
- Fair rent provisions
- Eviction only on specified grounds
- Right to purchase land by tenant

**U.P. Imposition of Ceiling on Land Holdings Act 1960:**
- Ceiling limits: Varying by family size and land quality
- Surplus land: Acquired and distributed
- Exemptions: Orchards, sugarcane farms, religious institutions

**Recent Developments:**
- Digital land records (Bhulekh UP)
- Online mutation and land record services
- Land acquisition reforms under RFCTLARR 2013

**Landmark Cases:**
- State of UP v. Renusagar (1988) - Land ceiling
- Ram Lakhan v. State of UP (2016) - Tenancy rights

**PYQ (UP PCS-J 2023):** Discuss the land ceiling provisions under U.P. law. What are the exemptions?
summary: U.P. land reforms: zamindari abolition, ceiling on holdings, consolidation. Tenancy: protected rights, fair rent, eviction grounds. Ceiling Act 1960 with exemptions. Digital land records.
